news_summary: Today's environmental news highlights innovative strides in sustainable fashion, engaging community efforts to protect wildlife, Colombia's leadership in climate action, and advancements in eco-friendly technology design.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité environnementale d'aujourd'hui met en avant les avancées innovantes dans le domaine de la mode durable, les initiatives communautaires visant à protéger la faune sauvage, le rôle de premier plan de la Colombie dans la lutte contre le changement climatique, ainsi que les progrès réalisés en matière de conception de technologies respectueuses de l'environnement.
news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan los avances innovadores en la moda sostenible, las iniciativas comunitarias para proteger la fauna silvestre, el liderazgo de Colombia en la lucha contra el cambio climático y los avances en el diseño de tecnologías respetuosas con el medio ambiente.

articles.title: A Stylish Investment: Making Fashion Sustainable
articles.summary:
Fashion is a major sustainability challenge in the global economy, and for most of the...
The post A Stylish Investment: Making Fashion Sustainable appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Fashion is a major sustainability challenge in the global economy, and for most of the last decade, it has faced little regulation. That is starting to change. In the past eighteen months, California passed the first U.S. law for extended producer responsibility (EPR) for textiles, France approved strict anti-fast-fashion laws, and the EU set a 2027 deadline for all member states to have a textile EPR program.

Every second, a garbage truck’s worth of clothing ends up in a landfill or is burned somewhere in the world. This isn’t just a figure of speech. The fashion industry produces about 92 million metric tons of waste each year, and if nothing changes, that number could reach 148 million metric tons by 2030.

Meanwhile, the resale market is growing about three times faster than traditional retail. The industry still has a long way to go, but for the first time, there are real systems in place to hold it accountable.

The Scale of the Problem

How big is fashion’s impact? It’s large, debated, and still growing. The fashion industry is responsible for 8 to 10 percent of global greenhouse gas emissions, according to the UN Environment Programme. While experts debate the exact numbers, everyone agrees the problem is getting worse.

The Apparel Impact Institute, a nonprofit supported by brands like H&M, Target, PVH, and Lululemon, reported that apparel sector emissions rose by 7.5 percent in 2023. This was the first yearly increase since 2019, and the group linked it to overproduction, ultra-fast fashion, and more use of virgin polyester, which now accounts for 57 percent of global fiber production.

No matter which numbers you believe, the trend is troubling. Each year, 80 to 100 billion new garments are made. Clothing production has doubled since 2000, and people now wear each item 36 percent fewer times before throwing it away. Synthetic fibers, mostly polyester made from fossil fuels, make up about 57 percent of global fiber production and are expected to increase.

The amount of water used in fashion is huge, even by industrial standards. Making one cotton T-shirt takes about 2,700 liters of water, which could provide drinking water for one person for 900 days. Producing a pair of jeans uses about 7,500 liters. Textile dyeing and treatment is the world’s second-largest source of water pollution, causing about 20 percent of industrial water pollution. ic clothing also sheds microplastics every time it’s washed. The IUCN has estimated that about 35 percent of primary microplastics in the ocean originate from synthetic textiles like polyester, nylon, and acrylic, though the total volume keeps rising as synthetic usage increases.

After technology manufacturing, garment production is still one of the industries most affected by modern slavery and child labor, according to International Labour Organization data. These problems are most common in the early stages of production, such as cotton farms, dye houses, and fabric mills, which are less visible than the brand-name factories.

Fast Fashion, Faster: The Shein and Temu Problem

In the last five years, a new category called ultra-fast fashion has emerged, making older models like Zara and H&M seem slow by comparison. Platforms such as Shein and Temu add thousands of new styles daily, produce items on demand in Chinese factories, and ship directly to customers around the world.

The environmental impact is severe. Shein’s own reports show its greenhouse gas emissions nearly doubled from 2022 to 2023, reaching 16.7 million metric tons of CO₂ equivalent. That’s almost as much as Inditex, Zara’s parent company, which is five times bigger by revenue. In 2024, Shein’s transportation emissions alone were over 8.5 million metric tons, more than three times Inditex’s. Temu hasn’t shared its emissions data, but third-party estimates put its yearly footprint between 4 and 6 million metric tons of CO₂e, mostly from shipping over a million air-freight parcels each day.

These business models not only pass environmental costs onto others, they rely on it. This is the main reason behind the push for new regulations.

The New Regulatory Landscape

For most of modern fashion history, sustainability promises have been voluntary, hard to verify, and mostly ineffective. That is finally starting to change. Three recent developments in the past eighteen months are especially important to watch..

California’s Responsible Textile Recovery Act (SB 707)

Governor Gavin Newsom signed SB 707 into law in September 2024, making California the first U.S. state with extended producer responsibility for textiles. The law shifts responsibility for end-of-use management of apparel, footwear, and household textiles from consumers and municipalities to the companies that put the products on the market. Producers with less than $1 million in annual global revenue are exempt; everyone else must join a state-approved Producer Responsibility Organization (PRO) that will finance collection, repair, reuse, sorting, and recycling.

Implementation is staged. On February 27, 2026, CalRecycle selected Landbell USA as California’s textile PRO. Producers must register with the PRO by July 1, 2026. A statewide needs assessment runs through 2027, final implementing regulations are due by July 2028, and full enforcement begins July 1, 2030, with fines of up to $50,000 per day for noncompliance.

France’s Anti–Fast Fashion Law

In June 2025, the French Senate passed the most aggressive anti-fast-fashion legislation in the world by a vote of 337 to 1. The law imposes a per-item eco-tax starting at €5 and rising to €10 by 2030 (capped at 50 percent of retail price), bans advertising and influencer marketing of ultra-fast-fashion brands, requires point-of-sale environmental disclosures including carbon footprint and durability data, and carries fines of up to €100,000 for violating the ad ban. Revenue is directed to French sustainable-fashion producers.

The law is clearly aimed at Shein and Temu. In November 2025, French authorities requested that Shein’s fast-fashion platform be suspended for three months over the sale of illicit products — days after Shein opened its first physical retail store in Paris. The European Commission issued a detailed opinion on the French law in September 2025; other EU member states are watching.

The EU Waste Framework Directive

Under revisions to the EU Waste Framework Directive, every member state was required to have separate textile waste collection in place by January 2025 and must have a fully operational textile EPR scheme by 2027. France’s EPR program, which has been operating since 2008, and the Netherlands (2023) are already live. Italy, Spain, and others have draft decrees in public consultation. Outside the EU, Switzerland, Australia, and Chile are developing national frameworks.

In the U.S., beyond California, New York’s Fashion Sustainability and Social Accountability Act (A4631) and Senate Bill S3217A both carried into the 2026 session. Washington State introduced HB 1420 in January 2025; as of March 2026, it remains in committee. None of these have passed.

The Resale Market Is Doing What Regulation Hasn’t

While policymakers work on new rules, consumers are already changing their habits. ThredUp’s 2025 Resale Report says the U.S. secondhand clothing market grew by 14 percent in 2024, five times faster than traditional retail. It’s expected to reach $74 billion by 2029. Globally, the secondhand market could hit $367 billion by 2029, growing 2.7 times faster than the overall apparel market.

There is a clear generational divide. In 2024, 58 percent of U.S. consumers bought secondhand clothing. Among those aged 18 to 44, 48 percent now choose secondhand first when shopping for clothes. Thirty-nine percent of younger shoppers have bought secondhand items through social platforms like Instagram or TikTok Shop.

Resale alone won’t solve fashion’s environmental impact. Extending a garment’s life only helps if it replaces a new purchase. Still, this is the biggest shift in consumer behavior the industry has seen in a generation.

What Sustainable Fashion Actually Means

Sustainable fashion means having a supply chain that is responsible for both the environment and people at every stage. In practice, this includes using fibers that need less water, fewer chemicals, and create lower emissions; manufacturing with renewable energy; ensuring fair wages and safe working conditions; making products that last and can be repaired; and recycling materials into new clothes instead of turning them into insulation or sending them to landfills in places like Ghana or Chile.

It’s a long list, and no brand meets every standard. Still, more brands are making real progress. Patagonia, Eileen Fisher, and Pangaia share detailed impact reports that are checked by outside experts. Brands using leftover fabrics, made-to-order production, and closed-loop recycling are slowly growing. Certifications like Global Organic Textile Standard (GOTS) for organic fibers, Fair Trade Certified for labor, and bluesign for chemical management are meaningful when you see them on a label.

Fashion is still the most greenwashed part of the consumer goods industry. Words like “conscious,” “eco,” and “sustainable” aren’t regulated in the U.S. What really matters are specific certifications, published supply-chain data, and third-party audits—not marketing slogans.

Take Action At Home

Individual choices won’t fix fashion’s big problems, but they do influence demand. That demand can drive companies and lawmakers to make changes. Here are some practical steps, ranked by impact:

Buy less, buy better. The single most impactful choice is reducing the amount of new clothing entering your closet. A capsule wardrobe of durable, versatile pieces worn many times beats any “sustainable” label on a fast-fashion cycle.

The single most impactful choice is reducing the amount of new clothing entering your closet. A capsule wardrobe of durable, versatile pieces worn many times beats any “sustainable” label on a fast-fashion cycle. Shop secondhand first. ThredUp, Poshmark, Depop, The RealReal, Vinted, and local thrift and consignment stores now offer selection and convenience comparable to traditional retail.

ThredUp, Poshmark, Depop, The RealReal, Vinted, and local thrift and consignment stores now offer selection and convenience comparable to traditional retail. Get familiar with clothing materials. Natural fibers like organic cotton, linen, hemp, and wool usually have a smaller environmental impact at the end of their life than synthetics. Recycled polyester is better than new polyester, but it still releases microfibers.

Natural fibers like organic cotton, linen, hemp, and wool usually have a smaller environmental impact at the end of their life than synthetics. Recycled polyester is better than new polyester, but it still releases microfibers. Use a microfiber filter. Tools like the Guppyfriend wash bag or washing machine filters can catch a lot of synthetic microfibers before they enter the water system.

Tools like the Guppyfriend wash bag or washing machine filters can catch a lot of synthetic microfibers before they enter the water system. Repair before replacing. Visible mending, basic tailoring, and simple patches can extend a garment’s life by years.

Visible mending, basic tailoring, and simple patches can extend a garment’s life by years. Take care of your clothes so they last longer. Wash them in cold water, air-dry when you can, and avoid the dry cleaner unless it’s necessary. These steps help reduce emissions and wear on your clothes.

Wash them in cold water, air-dry when you can, and avoid the dry cleaner unless it’s necessary. These steps help reduce emissions and wear on your clothes. Recycle clothes instead of throwing them away. When something can’t be worn anymore, look for textile recycling options using Earth911’s recycling locator or a store take-back program. Sending clothes to a landfill should be the last resort.

When something can’t be worn anymore, look for textile recycling options using Earth911’s recycling locator or a store take-back program. Sending clothes to a landfill should be the last resort. Support new policies. Laws about textile EPR, supply-chain transparency, and anti-greenwashing are being considered in many states. These laws are more likely to pass when people contact their representatives.

Fashion is one of the most obvious ways the global economy affects our daily lives. Because it’s so visible, everyone is part of the problem—but it also means that when change happens, it’s easy to notice.

Editor’ Note: Originally written by Gemma Alexander on April 8, 2022, this article was substantially updated in April 2026.
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant regulatory and market shifts toward sustainable fashion, including new laws in California, France, and the EU that impose extended producer responsibility and anti-fast-fashion measures. It highlights the growing resale market as a positive consumer-driven change and provides actionable steps for individuals. These developments represent meaningful, broad-scale progress addressing the environmental impact of the fashion industry, a major global sustainability challenge.
articles.personality_title: New laws and resale growth push fashion toward sustainability
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The fashion industry creates a huge amount of waste and pollution. Every year, millions of tons of clothes are thrown away, causing problems like water pollution and greenhouse gas emissions. For a long time, there were few rules to make fashion companies responsible for this damage.

**What happened** – Recently, important new laws have been passed to change this. California introduced a law that makes clothing companies responsible for recycling and managing used clothes. France passed strict rules against fast fashion, including taxes and advertising bans on very cheap, quickly made clothes. The European Union requires all its countries to have programs to collect and recycle textiles by 2027. At the same time, people are buying more secondhand clothes, with the resale market growing much faster than regular clothing sales.

**Impact** – These changes are important because fashion is one of the biggest sources of pollution and waste in the world. For example, making a single cotton T-shirt uses 2,700 liters of water. The new laws make companies responsible for the full life of their clothes, encouraging them to produce less waste and pollution. The growth of secondhand shopping shows consumers want more sustainable options, which can reduce the number of new clothes made.

**What's next step** – The new laws will start being enforced over the next few years, with California’s rules fully active by 2030 and the EU’s programs by 2027. Other places like New York and Washington are also considering similar laws. Meanwhile, consumers can help by buying fewer new clothes, choosing secondhand items, repairing clothes, and recycling textiles. These efforts together can push the fashion industry toward being more responsible and less harmful.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New laws and growing secondhand shopping are creating real change to reduce fashion’s environmental harm and encourage sustainable clothing choices.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles lois et la croissance de la revente poussent la mode vers la durabilité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'industrie de la mode génère une énorme quantité de déchets et de pollution. Chaque année, des millions de tonnes de vêtements sont jetés, causant des problèmes comme la pollution de l'eau et les émissions de gaz à effet de serre. Pendant longtemps, peu de règles obligeaient les entreprises de mode à être responsables de ces dégâts.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Récemment, des lois importantes ont été adoptées pour changer cela. La Californie a introduit une loi qui rend les entreprises de vêtements responsables du recyclage et de la gestion des vêtements usagés. La France a adopté des règles strictes contre la mode rapide, incluant des taxes et des interdictions de publicité sur les vêtements très bon marché et rapidement fabriqués. L'Union européenne exige que tous ses pays aient des programmes de collecte et de recyclage des textiles d'ici 2027. En même temps, les achats de vêtements d'occasion augmentent rapidement.

**Impact** – Ces changements sont importants car la mode est une des plus grandes sources de pollution et de déchets au monde. Par exemple, fabriquer un seul T-shirt en coton utilise 2 700 litres d'eau. Les nouvelles lois obligent les entreprises à être responsables de la vie complète de leurs vêtements, les encourageant à produire moins de déchets et de pollution. La croissance des achats d'occasion montre que les consommateurs veulent des options plus durables, ce qui peut réduire la production de nouveaux vêtements.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces nouvelles lois seront appliquées dans les prochaines années, avec la Californie totalement opérationnelle d'ici 2030 et les programmes européens d'ici 2027. D'autres endroits comme New York et Washington envisagent des lois similaires. Pendant ce temps, les consommateurs peuvent aider en achetant moins de vêtements neufs, en choisissant des articles d'occasion, en réparant leurs vêtements et en recyclant les textiles. Ces efforts ensemble peuvent pousser l'industrie de la mode à être plus responsable et moins nocive.

**Phrase clé** – De nouvelles lois et la croissance des achats d'occasion créent un vrai changement pour réduire les dégâts environnementaux de la mode et encourager des choix vestimentaires durables.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas leyes y crecimiento de la reventa impulsan la moda hacia la sostenibilidad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La industria de la moda genera una gran cantidad de residuos y contaminación. Cada año, se tiran millones de toneladas de ropa, causando problemas como la contaminación del agua y emisiones de gases de efecto invernadero. Durante mucho tiempo, hubo pocas reglas que hicieran responsables a las empresas de moda por estos daños.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, se aprobaron leyes importantes para cambiar esto. California introdujo una ley que hace a las compañías de ropa responsables del reciclaje y manejo de prendas usadas. Francia aprobó reglas estrictas contra la moda rápida, incluyendo impuestos y prohibiciones de publicidad para ropa muy barata y producida rápidamente. La Unión Europea exige que todos sus países tengan programas para recolectar y reciclar textiles para 2027. Al mismo tiempo, la compra de ropa de segunda mano está creciendo rápidamente.

**Impacto** – Estos cambios son importantes porque la moda es una de las mayores fuentes de contaminación y residuos en el mundo. Por ejemplo, producir una camiseta de algodón usa 2,700 litros de agua. Las nuevas leyes hacen que las empresas sean responsables por toda la vida de sus prendas, animándolas a producir menos residuos y contaminación. El crecimiento de la compra de segunda mano muestra que los consumidores buscan opciones más sostenibles, lo que puede reducir la fabricación de ropa nueva.

**Próximo paso** – Las nuevas leyes comenzarán a aplicarse en los próximos años, con las reglas de California activas para 2030 y los programas de la UE para 2027. Otros lugares como Nueva York y Washington también están considerando leyes similares. Mientras tanto, los consumidores pueden ayudar comprando menos ropa nueva, eligiendo prendas de segunda mano, reparando ropa y reciclando textiles. Estos esfuerzos juntos pueden impulsar a la industria de la moda a ser más responsable y menos dañina.

**Resumen en una frase** – Nuevas leyes y el crecimiento de la compra de segunda mano están generando cambios reales para reducir el daño ambiental de la moda y fomentar elecciones de ropa sostenibles.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a flowing river made of colorful textiles weaving through a landscape where clothing items transform into thriving plants and recycled fibers, surrounded by gentle silhouettes of sewing tools and fabric scraps symbolizing repair and reuse, all rendered in soft, natural hues.

articles.title: One night a year, humans command this march of frogs and salamanders
articles.content:
On a Tuesday night in April, beneath a sky mottled with clouds, a slick stretch of road in Cumberland, Maine, erupted in sound. It started with a few high-pitched chirps, like the coos of chicks. Within minutes, dozens, then hundreds more joined a chorus punctuated by low clucks. By the time the sun dipped below the horizon and rain began to splatter the pavement, the sound had risen to a din. Cars stopped on the shoulder and people spilled onto the road wearing neon vests and waving bright flashlights. They fanned out, and raised their voices as they spoke, like guests at a bustling cocktail party.

“I got a big one!” called a youngster in a yellow raincoat. She held out her hand for other volunteers who crowded around her. A yellow-spotted salamander about 9 inches long stretched across her gloved palm, its slick tail draped between her fingers.

Each year in New England, on the first warm, wet night of spring, when the ground has thawed, and the temperature is just right, armies of frogs and maelstroms of salamanders emerge from the woods. They hop and undulate through the night, following the same routes their ancestors traveled to the vernal pools of their birth, where they lay their eggs, chirping and clucking all the while.

“They’re calling to the ones that are still in the woods, telling them to come,” said Penny Asherman, who leads the Chebeague and Cumberland Land Trust.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

For the past decade, “Big Night” has drawn dozens of people who drop everything at a moment’s notice to help the amphibians migrate safely. But climate change is scrambling that ancient trek. The journey begins less predictably, has grown deadlier, and become more tenuous as the seasonal wetlands they depend on are transformed by climate change. That has prompted the volunteers to become citizen scientists, tracking when the animals emerge and how many survive. Coordinated by Maine Big Night, the effort, which came on April 14 this year, is generating data that is reshaping how communities think about culverts, road maintenance, and other infrastructure.

Volunteers hold a yellow spotted salamander after ferrying it across the road. Grace Benninghoff

In the past, these amphibian protectors were little more than crossing guards, shepherding the tiny creatures to safety. But a nonprofit formed in 2018, Maine Big Night, has asked them to meticulously document what happens along these migration paths. This year, more than 1,200 observers at 650 migration sites statewide submitted observations.

Tim Kaijala has been a regular for seven years along with his children, Theo, 10, and Kai, 8. “The data side is pretty cool,” he said. “When we first came, it was just bringing frogs and salamanders over, but the last couple years it’s been more about counting and keeping track.”

As he spoke, Theo and Kai peered into a pool, watching a wood frog they’d helped across the road kick through the clear water. “Remember that one time, Theo,” Kai said, looking at her brother.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“Oh yeah,” he said.

“Tell it,” she urged.

“One time there was a car coming down, and I ran out and saved the peeper,” he paused, solemnly. “I do not want any peepers to die. If I stepped on one, I would never forgive myself.”

When data last year showed that eight out of 10 amphibians were hit by motorists in Orono, at the state’s most ecologically diverse migration site, Big Night worked with city officials to secure a grant for cameras and fencing that guide the animals toward an existing culvert beneath the road. When the group also saw rising numbers with edema linked to road salt runoff, it pushed for alternative deicing methods, including pickle juice.

Greg LeClair founded Maine Big Night. By day, he’s a municipal planning biologist for the Maine Department of Inland Fisheries and Wildlife. He started the organization because he knew data collection would be essential for protecting the amphibians he’s adored since childhood.

A wood frog considers taking a leap into a vernal pool after being helped across the road. Grace Benninghoff

It’s easy to see why. Wood frogs are palm-sized, dappled brown creatures with dark markings smattering the skin around their wide eyes. Spring peepers are the size of a thumb and camouflage so well with the leaf litter of the forest floor that they’re nearly invisible until they unleash calls that echo through the night.

But loving them and saving them are two different things.

“I knew that in order to make change, you needed data, especially when we’re talking about critters folks aren’t as keen on,” LeClair said. Conserving land and installing culverts, two effective ways to protect the amphibians, aren’t cheap. “Nobody will give you the money unless you have data,” he said. “That money for infrastructure and conservation is not just floating around.”

Trouble is, little data exists on amphibian migration patterns. They’re small, spend most of the year burrowed in the woods, and are hard to track. “Any time anyone has a collision with a deer or moose and an insurance claim is filed, a data point is collected, but nobody files an insurance claim when they hit a frog.”

Good data does more than help amphibian advocates win protections. It ensures conservationists spend limited resources where they’ll do the most good. The fencing project in Orono is one example. For years, scientists thought specialized culverts were the only reliable way to get the hoppers and creepers off roads. But cameras there have already captured frogs and salamanders using an existing crossing. If the new fencing the town recently installed proves nearly as effective, it could save tens of thousands of dollars.

Protecting amphibians matters far beyond frogs and salamanders themselves. They are foundational to New England’s food web. Eggs, larvae, and adults all sustain a surprising range of animals from owls and herons to foxes and even moose. “If you remove one piece of the puzzle or two, you don’t know which piece could kick the whole system out of whack,” said Sally Stockwell of Maine Audubon. “But there are huge trickle-down impacts when you lose the base of the food chain.”

Amphibians are also particularly vulnerable to climate change. They can’t regulate their body temperature, and they need moisture to move. In the winter, when they burrow into the soil to stay warm, dwindling snowpack can leave them without enough insulation, and they freeze to death. Unusually warm winter days can draw them out of their hiding places, and the return of freezing temperatures kills them. A dry spring or sudden heatwave can dry out the vernal pools where they lay their eggs, killing the next generation.

As the climate warms, fungi adapted to warmer, drier conditions are becoming a greater threat. Among them is the deadly chytrid fungus, which grows on amphibians’ skin, impairs their ability to breathe, and has been seen more frequently in recent years.

Yet we remain their greatest threat. Development eliminates their habitat, and cars kill untold numbers of them. That is why data is so important: It reveals what would otherwise go unseen. In Cumberland this year, volunteers counted 10 species crossing, including more than 100 spring peepers, 34 wood frogs, and 18 spotted salamanders. Just nine amphibians were found dead. “Anything we can do to reduce mortality is a benefit,” said Stockwell.

And on that rainy night in April, volunteers did all they could. Until nearly midnight, children and parents, college kids, and retirees patrolled the road and forest beyond, jotting notes on clipboards and ferrying frogs to safety in Tupperware. They paused only to watch as the tiny cold-blooded critters stretched their limbs and swam, sometimes bobbing at the surface to call — at shocking volume — to the ones still in the woods.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful community-driven conservation effort to protect amphibians during their vulnerable migration, with significant real-world impact on local biodiversity and ecosystem health. It highlights citizen science generating valuable data that informs infrastructure and environmental policy changes, addressing climate change threats and habitat loss. The story is focused, detailed, and shows broad ecological importance beyond a small niche.
articles.personality_title: Volunteers protect migrating frogs and salamanders in Maine each spring
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every spring in New England, frogs and salamanders make a special journey from the woods to the pools where they were born. This migration happens on warm, rainy nights when the animals leave their homes to lay eggs. Roads can be dangerous for these small creatures during their trip.

**What happened** – In Cumberland, Maine, on a rainy April night, more than 1,200 volunteers across the state gathered to help frogs and salamanders cross roads safely. They carried the animals across busy streets, recorded how many crossed, and noted any dangers they faced. This event, called “Big Night,” is organized by a group named Maine Big Night, which started in 2018 to protect these animals and collect important data.

**Impact** – The volunteers’ work is more than just saving animals—it helps scientists and city officials understand when and where amphibians migrate. This information has led to safer road designs, like fences and tunnels that guide animals away from cars. It also raises awareness about threats from climate change, such as drying pools and harmful fungi. Protecting these amphibians helps keep the local environment balanced since many animals rely on them for food.

**What's next step** – The data collected will continue to guide towns in making better plans to protect amphibians. Volunteers will keep monitoring migration patterns, especially as climate change alters their habits. This ongoing effort could lead to more cost-effective solutions, like using existing tunnels instead of building new ones, and encourage safer road care practices.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Communities in Maine are working together each spring to safely guide frogs and salamanders across roads, using citizen science to protect these important animals and their habitats.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des bénévoles protègent les grenouilles et salamandres migrantes dans le Maine chaque printemps
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque printemps en Nouvelle-Angleterre, les grenouilles et salamandres entreprennent un voyage spécial des bois vers les étangs où elles sont nées. Cette migration a lieu lors de nuits chaudes et pluvieuses, quand ces animaux quittent leur habitat pour pondre leurs œufs. Les routes sont dangereuses pour eux pendant ce trajet.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – À Cumberland, dans le Maine, lors d'une nuit pluvieuse en avril, plus de 1 200 bénévoles à travers l'État se sont réunis pour aider les grenouilles et salamandres à traverser les routes en toute sécurité. Ils transportaient les animaux, comptaient combien traversaient et notaient les dangers rencontrés. Cet événement, appelé « Big Night », est organisé par le groupe Maine Big Night, créé en 2018 pour protéger ces animaux et recueillir des données importantes.

**Impact** – Le travail des bénévoles ne se limite pas à sauver des animaux : il aide les scientifiques et les autorités locales à mieux comprendre quand et où les amphibiens migrent. Ces informations ont permis d'améliorer la sécurité routière avec des clôtures et des tunnels guidant les animaux loin des voitures. Elles sensibilisent aussi aux menaces du changement climatique, comme l'assèchement des étangs et des champignons dangereux. Protéger ces amphibiens aide à maintenir l'équilibre de l'environnement local, car beaucoup d'animaux en dépendent pour se nourrir.

**Prochaine étape** – Les données recueillies guideront les villes pour mieux protéger les amphibiens. Les bénévoles continueront à surveiller les migrations, surtout avec les changements liés au climat. Cet effort permanent pourrait mener à des solutions plus économiques, comme utiliser des tunnels existants plutôt que d’en construire de nouveaux, et encourager des pratiques plus sûres pour l’entretien des routes.

**Une phrase clé** – Les communautés du Maine s’unissent chaque printemps pour guider en toute sécurité grenouilles et salamandres sur les routes, utilisant la science citoyenne pour protéger ces animaux essentiels et leurs habitats.
articles.personality_title_es: Voluntarios protegen a ranas y salamandras migrantes en Maine cada primavera
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada primavera en Nueva Inglaterra, las ranas y salamandras hacen un viaje especial desde el bosque hasta las charcas donde nacieron. Esta migración ocurre en noches cálidas y lluviosas, cuando los animales salen de sus hogares para poner huevos. Las carreteras pueden ser peligrosas para ellos durante este trayecto.

**Qué pasó** – En Cumberland, Maine, en una noche lluviosa de abril, más de 1,200 voluntarios en todo el estado se reunieron para ayudar a las ranas y salamandras a cruzar las calles con seguridad. Transportaron a los animales, contaron cuántos cruzaban y anotaron los peligros que enfrentaban. Este evento, llamado “Big Night”, es organizado por un grupo llamado Maine Big Night, fundado en 2018 para proteger a estos animales y recoger datos importantes.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de los voluntarios va más allá de salvar animales: ayuda a científicos y autoridades a entender cuándo y dónde migran los anfibios. Esta información ha llevado a diseñar carreteras más seguras, con cercas y túneles que guían a los animales lejos de los autos. También aumenta la conciencia sobre amenazas del cambio climático, como el secado de charcas y hongos dañinos. Proteger a estos anfibios ayuda a mantener el equilibrio del ecosistema local, ya que muchos animales dependen de ellos para alimentarse.

**Próximo paso** – Los datos recogidos seguirán ayudando a las ciudades a planear mejor la protección de los anfibios. Los voluntarios continuarán monitoreando las migraciones, especialmente porque el cambio climático está alterando sus hábitos. Este esfuerzo continuo podría llevar a soluciones más económicas, como usar túneles existentes en lugar de construir nuevos, y promover mejores prácticas para el cuidado de las carreteras.

**Una frase clave** – Las comunidades en Maine trabajan juntas cada primavera para guiar con seguridad a ranas y salamandras a través de las carreteras, usando ciencia ciudadana para proteger estos animales importantes y sus hábitats.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing nighttime forest scene with a winding, rain-slicked road where silhouettes of frogs and yellow-spotted salamanders move steadily across, guided by warm pools of light from scattered lanterns and neon vests glowing gently in the mist, while delicate raindrops fall under a clouded sky, capturing the quiet urgency and care of a community safeguarding the amphibian migration.

articles.title: Colombia hosts climate talks in bid to lead global transition away from fossil fuels
articles.summary: Colombian President Gustavo Petro vowed to “wean” the country’s economy off oil and coal when he was elected in 2022. But as his term draws to a close – with the first round of a presidential election set for May 31 – Petro’s ambitious energy transition plan remains unfulfilled, with the country still reliant on fossil-fuel production.
articles.content:
Activists participate in a demonstration during a conference aimed at transitioning away from fossil fuels Monday, April 27, 2026, in Santa Marta, Colombia.

It could be described as the “challenge of the century”.

Energy ministers from some 50 countries are gathering for a two-day summit from April 28-29 in the city of Santa Marta, at the foot of Colombia’s Sierra Nevada mountain range, to address the crucial question of how to move away from fossil fuels – the main drivers of global warming – once and for all.

Organised by Bogota, the summit was prompted by a sobering realisation: when it comes to tackling gas, oil and coal production, the major UN COP meetings are no longer enough. This became clear at the conclusion of COP28 in Dubai in 2023 after a landmark decision to adopt the convention’s final text mentioned the need to begin to “transition away from fossil fuels”.

But negotiations have stalled since then. Consensus has been thwarted with discussions systematically blocked by the major oil and gas-producing countries.

However, the Santa Marta conference may well break the deadlock in climate diplomacy. The question is no longer whether or not fossil fuels should be phased out but rather how the most ambitious countries wrestling with the challenges of climate change can achieve concrete solutions.

Read moreFrance's 'roadmap' to exit fossil fuels by 2050

‘What is more poisonous for humanity: cocaine, coal or oil?’

It is no coincidence that Colombia is hosting this unprecedented summit. In the small world of oil-producing nations, Gustavo Petro, the country’s first left-wing president, stands out as somewhat of an outsider. Since his inauguration in August 2022, he has consistently criticised the devastating "addiction" to oil that is harming the planet and pledged to transform Colombia, Latin America’s fourth-largest oil exporter, into an economy "free of oil and coal".

A month later, during his first speech to the UN General Assembly, Petro did not shy away from controversy. “What is more poisonous for humanity: cocaine, coal or oil?" he asked.

At COP28 in Dubai, capital of the oil-rich United Arab Emirates, only three months after his UN address, he summed up his philosophy.

“Some will ask how I can contemplate such economic suicide [by doing away with fossil fuels], given that we depend on oil and coal,” he said.

"But today we are witnessing a massive clash between fossil capital and human life, and we must choose a side. We are choosing the side of life."

Renewable energy programmes

Petro’s verbal facility and quickfire soundbites have translated into action on the ground, even when they’ve ruffled the feathers of his most strident opponents. Shortly after being elected, he increased taxes on hydrocarbons, banned all new oil exploration contracts and launched a campaign promoting “the country’s rich biodiversity” rather than its fossil fuel reserves.

In 2025 his environment minister, Irene Velez Torres, declared the “Colombian Amazon free from all extractive exploitation”.

As for hydraulic fracturing – or fracking as it is commonly known – a process used to extract shale gas, all new projects have been suspended until further notice while a legislative standoff is under way between the government and Congress over moves to ban them permanently.

At the same time, the government has launched various renewable energy programmes, such as "Colombia Solar". With a budget of $2 billion (€1.7 billion) up to 2030, its goal is to install solar farms and rooftop panels, especially in the Caribbean region, which enjoys year-round sunshine.

Experts acknowledge Petro has made significant strides in transitioning the country’s energy supplies away from fossil fuels.

Watch more'We represent a new force': Nearly 60 nations push ahead with fossil fuel exit

While the majority of Colombia’s electricity remains hydroelectric, the proportion of solar and wind power has surged within the country’s energy mix. Its share has risen from 2% in 2022 to 17% in 2026, according to government figures. Last year, solar power overtook coal for the first time.

"The year 2025 marked the first time that exports in terms of remittances and foreign exchange earnings from coffee exceeded those from coal. And 2025 was also the first year in which Colombia generated more energy from unconventional renewable sources, particularly solar power, than from coal," said Velez Torres, speaking on the sidelines of the Santa Marta summit.

Nearly 30% of the country’s exports

But despite the minister’s efforts, oil and gas still account for 4% of GDP and 30% of exports – meaning the country is far from shaking off the shackles of fossil fuel dependency.

“Oil remains a driving force behind the Colombian economy,” Tomas Gonzalez, director of the Regional Centre for Energy Studies (CREE), told the newspaper El Tiempo. “It still accounts for a third of exports and brings in nearly $15 billion for the Colombian state. In a country running a deficit like Colombia, oil revenues are essential for the state’s finances,” the economist added.

"There has been progress," but this does not amount to "a genuine energy transition", Oscar Vanegas, a professor of economics at the Industrial University of Santander, told AFP.

Moving away from fossil fuels "will take several decades", acknowledges energy planning specialist Ismael Suescun, explaining that “it is not just a matter of stopping the exploration and extraction of hydrocarbons”. It is a matter of “being aware of the gradual nature” required to transition the energy sector of a country whose budget deficit is high and where revenues remain tethered to hydrocarbons.

A symbol for how difficult that transition is likely to be can be found just 200 kilometres away from the Santa Marta talks. Operated by the multinational Glencore, the Cerrejon mine is one of the world’s largest open-cast coal mines and is regularly criticised for its environmental and social impacts.

Climate diplomacy

In hosting this conference just a few weeks before the end of his presidential term, Petro is undoubtedly hoping to reach beyond his country’s borders and once again position himself as a leader on the diplomatic stage in the global fight against fossil fuels.

In 2023, Colombia became the first non-island, oil-producing country to commit to the draft treaty on the non-proliferation of fossil fuels. Launched by a group of Pacific island nations, the initiative received the backing of the European Parliament, the World Health Organization and 100 cities and local governments, and aims to persuade states to commit to energy transition.

Last year, Petro was the first to publicly denounce the failure of fossil fuel negotiations at COP30 in Belem, Brazil. Today, he may well hope to be the first to spearhead a new diplomatic chapter by championing the first international scheme to regulate, or limit, the production of hydrocarbons and coal. A follow-up conference planned for Tuvalu in autumn – on the sidelines of a meeting of heads of state ahead of COP31 – could well provide him with the chance to do so.

This article has been adapted from the original in French by Nicole Trian.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on Colombia hosting a major international summit aimed at transitioning away from fossil fuels, highlighting concrete government actions and renewable energy progress with broad global climate implications. It focuses on a significant environmental policy and diplomatic effort with detailed context on Colombia's challenges and achievements in reducing fossil fuel dependency, making it a meaningful and inspiring story about climate action.
articles.personality_title: Colombia leads global talks to move away from fossil fuels
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Colombia is one of the world’s oil and coal producers, relying heavily on these fossil fuels for its economy. President Gustavo Petro, elected in 2022, promised to reduce this dependence and shift the country toward cleaner energy. However, the transition is challenging since fossil fuels still make up a large part of Colombia’s exports and government income.

**What happened** – On April 28-29, 2026, Colombia hosted a two-day international summit in Santa Marta. Energy ministers from about 50 countries gathered to discuss how to stop using fossil fuels worldwide. This meeting came after global talks stalled following the 2023 United Nations climate conference (COP28), where countries agreed they must begin moving away from fossil fuels but struggled to agree on how.

**Impact** – Colombia’s leadership is important because it combines strong climate goals with real actions. Since 2022, the government has stopped new oil exploration, increased taxes on fossil fuels, protected the Amazon from mining, and invested $2 billion in solar energy projects. These efforts have helped solar and wind power grow from 2% to 17% of Colombia’s electricity in just four years. For the first time, solar energy produced more power than coal. This shows that even countries dependent on fossil fuels can start making big changes.

**What’s next step** – The summit aims to break the deadlock in global fossil fuel talks and push countries to create real plans for reducing oil, gas, and coal use. Colombia plans to continue leading these efforts, with another conference scheduled in Tuvalu later in 2026. Meanwhile, the country will keep expanding renewable energy while managing the economic challenges of moving away from fossil fuels.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Colombia is setting an example by hosting global talks and making real progress to reduce fossil fuel use, showing how change is possible even in countries tied to oil and coal.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Colombie organise des pourparlers mondiaux pour réduire l’usage des combustibles fossiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Colombie est un important producteur de pétrole et de charbon, et son économie dépend beaucoup de ces combustibles fossiles. Le président Gustavo Petro, élu en 2022, a promis de réduire cette dépendance et de passer à une énergie plus propre. Mais cette transition est difficile car les combustibles fossiles représentent encore une grande part des exportations et des revenus du pays.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les 28 et 29 avril 2026, la Colombie a accueilli un sommet international de deux jours à Santa Marta. Des ministres de l’énergie d’environ 50 pays se sont réunis pour discuter de la manière de cesser d’utiliser les combustibles fossiles dans le monde. Cette réunion intervient après un blocage des négociations mondiales suite à la conférence climat de l’ONU (COP28) en 2023, où les pays ont reconnu la nécessité de réduire les combustibles fossiles mais n’ont pas réussi à s’entendre sur les modalités.

**Impact** – Le leadership de la Colombie est important car il combine des objectifs climatiques forts et des actions concrètes. Depuis 2022, le gouvernement a arrêté toute nouvelle exploration pétrolière, augmenté les taxes sur les combustibles fossiles, protégé l’Amazonie contre l’exploitation minière, et investi 2 milliards de dollars dans des projets solaires. Ces efforts ont permis à l’énergie solaire et éolienne de passer de 2 % à 17 % de l’électricité colombienne en quatre ans. Pour la première fois, l’énergie solaire a produit plus que le charbon. Cela montre que même les pays dépendants des combustibles fossiles peuvent commencer à changer.

**Prochaine étape** – Le sommet vise à débloquer les négociations mondiales sur les combustibles fossiles et à pousser les pays à établir de vrais plans pour réduire l’utilisation du pétrole, du gaz et du charbon. La Colombie prévoit de poursuivre ce leadership, avec un autre sommet prévu à Tuvalu fin 2026. Dans le même temps, le pays continuera d’étendre les énergies renouvelables tout en gérant les défis économiques liés au passage des combustibles fossiles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La Colombie montre l’exemple en organisant des pourparlers mondiaux et en progressant réellement vers la réduction des combustibles fossiles, prouvant que le changement est possible même pour les pays dépendants du pétrole et du charbon.
articles.personality_title_es: Colombia lidera conversaciones globales para reducir el uso de combustibles fósiles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Colombia es uno de los países que produce petróleo y carbón, y su economía depende mucho de estos combustibles fósiles. El presidente Gustavo Petro, elegido en 2022, prometió reducir esta dependencia y avanzar hacia energías más limpias. Sin embargo, la transición es difícil porque los combustibles fósiles aún representan una gran parte de las exportaciones y los ingresos del país.

**Qué pasó** – Del 28 al 29 de abril de 2026, Colombia organizó una cumbre internacional de dos días en Santa Marta. Ministros de energía de unos 50 países se reunieron para hablar sobre cómo dejar de usar combustibles fósiles en todo el mundo. Esta reunión llegó después de que las negociaciones globales se estancaran tras la conferencia climática de la ONU (COP28) en 2023, donde los países acordaron que deben comenzar a alejarse de los combustibles fósiles, pero no lograron ponerse de acuerdo en cómo hacerlo.

**Impacto** – El liderazgo de Colombia es importante porque combina metas ambientales fuertes con acciones reales. Desde 2022, el gobierno ha detenido nuevas exploraciones petroleras, aumentado impuestos a los combustibles fósiles, protegido la Amazonía de la minería, e invertido 2 mil millones de dólares en proyectos solares. Estos esfuerzos han hecho que la energía solar y eólica crezcan del 2 % al 17 % de la electricidad en Colombia en solo cuatro años. Por primera vez, la energía solar generó más electricidad que el carbón. Esto muestra que incluso los países dependientes de combustibles fósiles pueden empezar a cambiar.

**Próximo paso** – La cumbre busca desbloquear las negociaciones globales sobre combustibles fósiles y motivar a los países a crear planes concretos para reducir el uso de petróleo, gas y carbón. Colombia planea seguir liderando estos esfuerzos, con otra conferencia prevista en Tuvalu a finales de 2026. Mientras tanto, el país continuará aumentando las energías renovables mientras maneja los retos económicos de dejar los combustibles fósiles.

**Frase clave** – Colombia está dando un ejemplo al organizar conversaciones globales y avanzar en la reducción del uso de combustibles fósiles, demostrando que el cambio es posible incluso en países ligados al petróleo y al carbón.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a lush Colombian mountain landscape at the foot of the Sierra Nevada, where a large, glowing solar panel array and gentle wind turbines rise harmoniously alongside a peaceful river; in the foreground, silhouettes of diverse activists hold hands forming a circle around a symbolic cracked oil barrel transforming into blooming native flowers, all rendered in warm, earthy tones and soft natural colors conveying hope and transition away from fossil fuels.

articles.title: The chips in your phone are probably broken – and that's a good thing
articles.summary: Reports suggest that Apple is using defective chips originally destined for high-end devices to create its latest affordable laptop. Reusing partially broken chips is common practice for all device makers and produces less waste
articles.content:
Reports suggest that Apple is using defective chips originally destined for high-end devices to create its latest affordable laptop. Reusing partially broken chips is common practice for all device makers and produces less waste

Manufacturing computer chips is never a perfect process Apple

Apple may be ramping up its use of defective chips to manufacture its new low-cost laptop. That may sound bad, but it is actually an example of a common practice called “binning”, which reduces the cost and environmental impact of our smartphones and laptops.

The name originated in farming, where premium fruit and vegetables are sold to one type of customer, misshapen ones are set aside for others and the worst, perhaps even rotten, produce is used as animal feed. Everything has its purpose, separated into bins of different grades, with no waste. The same happens in semiconductor manufacturing.

Take Apple’s new MacBook Neo – it promises customers a more affordable Apple laptop option, using an A18 Pro system with five GPU cores. But the A18 Pro was previously used on the iPhone 16 Pro and had six GPU cores. Reports suggest that the reason for the discrepancy is that Apple is using leftover and binned A18 Pros that had a flaw in one core and making good use of chips that would have been disposed of otherwise. Apple didn’t respond to a request for comment, but experts told New Scientist that it is common practice for makers of everything from phones to cars and microwaves.

Owen Guy at Swansea University in the UK says that chips are made in batches of hundreds on a single 300-millimetre silicon wafer that contains trillions of individual transistors. Complex machinery performs thousands of individual operations on the wafer, setting down layers of circuit, insulation and various chemicals with thicknesses of just a few nanometres. If anything, it is more surprising that the fiendishly complex process works at all than that some chips will have flaws.

“There’s a small possibility that something could go wrong at each of those process steps,” says Guy.

The number of errors on a given wafer determines the yield rate – the number of chips that meet specifications. This might be as high as 99 per cent for relatively normal chips on silicon, which has been used to make chips since the 1960s, but rise with more ambitious chip designs and relatively newer and rarer substrate materials like silicon carbide or gallium nitride.

“The question then is what are the numbers of defects, and how serious are those defects? Because you can still have a chip, which works, which has some defects in it as long as they’re not what they call a killer defect,” says Guy.

Imagine a yield of 90 per cent, where 9 in 10 chips function exactly as planned. In that scenario, 1 in 10 chips will be binned. If there’s an error in a single core, this could mean labelling as a different product with five cores rather than six, or it could mean that it’s rated to work only at a lower voltage or frequency, or is specified as having a higher power use or running at a higher temperature. Somewhere there will be a customer who can make use of it.

Tony Kenyon at University College London says that to a user, there will be no indication that anything is wrong. Error-correction software will isolate broken transistors in a memory chip so that no data is ever lost, or route calculations around a damaged processor core so that no software ever crashes.

“If you lift the hood a little bit and look under that and see what’s going on at the level of individual transistors and gates and so on and so forth, there will be bits of the chip that maybe don’t function,” says Kenyon. “It’s very common. Everyone thinks that all chips are identical, and the reality is that they’re not.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a common semiconductor manufacturing practice called 'binning' that repurposes partially defective chips to produce affordable devices, significantly reducing electronic waste and environmental impact. This practice benefits a broad public by lowering costs and promoting sustainability in tech production, with clear and detailed explanation of the process and its positive implications.
articles.personality_title: Apple uses partly broken chips to build affordable laptops and cut waste
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Making computer chips is a very complex process. Sometimes, small flaws happen during production, so not all chips work perfectly. Instead of throwing these chips away, companies sort them by quality in a process called "binning." This helps reduce waste and costs.

**What happened** – Apple is reportedly using chips with minor defects in its new, affordable MacBook Neo laptop. These chips were originally made for higher-end devices like the iPhone 16 Pro but had one small problem, such as a broken graphics core. Apple uses these partly broken chips with five working cores instead of six, making good use of chips that might otherwise be discarded.

**Impact** – This approach lowers the price of Apple’s laptops, making them more accessible. It also reduces electronic waste because fewer chips are thrown away. Experts explain that tiny flaws in chips are common and that special software fixes or avoids problems, so users don’t notice any difference in performance.

**What's next step** – As chip-making becomes more advanced and demand for affordable devices grows, more companies may use binning to reuse chips with minor defects. This could lead to more affordable electronics and less environmental harm from wasted materials.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Apple’s use of partly broken chips in new laptops shows how smart recycling in technology can lower costs and reduce waste without affecting performance.
articles.personality_title_fr: Apple utilise des puces partiellement défectueuses pour fabriquer des ordinateurs portables abordables et réduire les déchets
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La fabrication des puces électroniques est un processus très complexe. Parfois, de petits défauts apparaissent, et toutes les puces ne fonctionnent pas parfaitement. Au lieu de les jeter, les entreprises les trient selon leur qualité dans un procédé appelé « binning ». Cela permet de réduire les déchets et les coûts.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Apple utiliserait des puces avec de petits défauts dans son nouvel ordinateur portable abordable, le MacBook Neo. Ces puces étaient initialement destinées à des appareils haut de gamme comme l’iPhone 16 Pro, mais avaient un problème mineur, par exemple un cœur graphique défectueux. Apple utilise ces puces partiellement cassées avec cinq cœurs fonctionnels au lieu de six, évitant ainsi le gaspillage.

**Impact** – Cette méthode permet de baisser le prix des ordinateurs Apple, les rendant plus accessibles. Elle réduit aussi les déchets électroniques car moins de puces sont jetées. Des experts expliquent que ces défauts sont courants et que des logiciels spéciaux corrigent ou contournent les problèmes, de sorte que les utilisateurs ne remarquent aucune différence.

**Prochaine étape** – À mesure que la fabrication des puces progresse et que la demande pour des appareils abordables augmente, davantage d’entreprises pourraient utiliser le binning pour réutiliser les puces légèrement défectueuses. Cela pourrait rendre l’électronique plus abordable et diminuer l’impact environnemental.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – L’utilisation par Apple de puces partiellement défectueuses dans ses nouveaux ordinateurs montre comment le recyclage intelligent en technologie peut réduire les coûts et les déchets sans nuire aux performances.
articles.personality_title_es: Apple usa chips parcialmente defectuosos para fabricar laptops accesibles y reducir desperdicios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Fabricar chips para computadoras es un proceso muy complicado. A veces, ocurren pequeños defectos y no todos los chips funcionan perfectamente. En lugar de desecharlos, las empresas los clasifican según su calidad en un proceso llamado "binning". Esto ayuda a reducir desperdicios y costos.

**Qué pasó** – Apple está usando chips con defectos menores en su nueva laptop económica, la MacBook Neo. Estos chips fueron hechos originalmente para dispositivos de alta gama como el iPhone 16 Pro, pero tenían un problema pequeño, como un núcleo gráfico roto. Apple usa estos chips parcialmente defectuosos con cinco núcleos en lugar de seis, aprovechando chips que podrían haber sido desechados.

**Impacto** – Este método reduce el precio de las laptops de Apple, haciéndolas más accesibles. También disminuye los desechos electrónicos porque se tiran menos chips. Los expertos explican que estos pequeños defectos son comunes y que el software especial corrige o evita problemas, por lo que los usuarios no notan diferencia en el rendimiento.

**Próximos pasos** – A medida que la fabricación de chips avanza y crece la demanda de dispositivos económicos, más empresas podrían usar el binning para reutilizar chips con defectos menores. Esto podría significar más dispositivos accesibles y menos daño al medio ambiente.

**Frase clave** – El uso de chips parcialmente defectuosos por parte de Apple en sus nuevas laptops muestra cómo el reciclaje inteligente en tecnología puede bajar costos y reducir desperdicios sin afectar el rendimiento.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, glowing silicon wafer gently cradled in soft hands made of light, with some tiny, bright circuit cores shining fully while one core glows dimmer but still connected, symbolizing imperfect yet valuable chips being lovingly repurposed; the background is a calm gradient of natural beige and soft blue tones, evoking care and sustainability.
